title: 1 3 Taylor Series Error Estimates
body:
1.3 TAYLOR SERIES AND ERROR ESTIMATION

                 The Taylor Series for functions of two variables:
                   The Taylor Series of f (x, y) about the point (a, b) is

                                                   ‚ąāf                 ‚ąāf
                   f (x, y) = f (a, b) + (x ‚ąí a)      (a, b) + (y ‚ąí b) (a, b)
                                                   ‚ąāx                 ‚ąāy
                       2
                                                    ‚ąā 2f                    2
                                                                                     
        1            2‚ąā f                                                2 ‚ąā f
      +       (x ‚ąí a)     (a, b) + 2(x ‚ąí a)(y ‚ąí b)       (a, b) + (y ‚ąí b)       (a, b)
        2!            ‚ąāx2                          ‚ąāx‚ąāy                    ‚ąāy 2
                                   + higher-order terms.


You have already seen how Taylor series are used to approximate complicated functions
with simple polynomials. Polynomials are very easy to work with and approximations of
this sort are often used in Engineering and the Sciences to simplify complex functions.
For example sin(x) is often approximated by x when the angle x is small.
Let‚Äôs remind ourselves of the Taylor series for a single variable.
Example 1 Find the Taylor series for g(x) = ex about the point x = 0.




                                                                                      2
                                                                         F1 + 1!x + x2! + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ F

                                              1
 For a function of two variables z = f (x, y), the Taylor Series is a little bit more compli-
cated because we are approximating a surface with simple polynomial functions in x and
y. Things to keep in mind are that:

‚ÄĘ The Taylor series will involve evaluating partial derivatives of the function f (x, y).

‚ÄĘ If we stop the Taylor Series for f (x, y) at some order, we call the resulting polynomial
expression the Taylor Series Approximation of the function f (x, y) near the point (a, b).

‚ÄĘ The Taylor Series about (a, b) will work best near (a, b)

‚ÄĘ The more terms you include the better your approximation will be. Higher-order terms
can be calculated using the binomial theorem.

Example 2 Find the Taylor series of f (x, y) = x4 y 3 about the point (1, 2) up to and
including quadratic terms.




              F 8 + 32(x ‚ąí 1) + 12(y ‚ąí 2) + 48(x ‚ąí 1)2 + 48(x ‚ąí 1)(y ‚ąí 2) + 6(y ‚ąí 2)2 F

                                              2
 Example 3 Calculate the Taylor series of f (x, y) = ex cos(y) about (a, b) = (0, 0) up
to and including quadratic terms. Compare the value of f (‚ąí0.1, 0.2) with its Taylor
approximation.




                                          F 1 + x + 21 (x2 ‚ąí y 2 ),0.88680091 vs 0.885 F

                                          3
                               ERROR ESTIMATES

The first three terms of the Taylor series are a linear approximation of f (x, y),

                                                   ‚ąāf                 ‚ąāf
                   f (x, y) ‚Čą f (a, b) + (x ‚ąí a)      (a, b) + (y ‚ąí b) (a, b)
                                                   ‚ąāx                 ‚ąāy
This can be rewritten as
                                                   ‚ąāf                 ‚ąāf
                   f (x, y) ‚ąí f (a, b) ‚Čą (x ‚ąí a)      (a, b) + (y ‚ąí b) (a, b)
                                                   ‚ąāx                 ‚ąāy
and hence
                                          ‚ąāf      ‚ąāf
                                   ‚ąÜf ‚Čą      ‚ąÜx +    ‚ąÜy.
                                          ‚ąāx      ‚ąāy
This lovely little equation gives you the change in f given a small change in x and y.
This is a very useful result, because it allows us to deal with the case when there is
uncertainty in the values of x and y. For example, if there are small errors ‚ąÜx and ‚ąÜy in
x and y, then we can use this formula to estimate the error ‚ąÜf in f (x, y)


Example 4 Suppose that temperature T is given by T (x, y) = x2 + y 3 . Find the
approximate error in T if x = 1, y = 2, ‚ąÜx = 0.1 and ‚ąÜy = ‚ąí0.3.




                                                                                F ‚ąí 3.4 F

                                               4
 In the example given, we knew the exact value and sign of the errors ‚ąÜx and ‚ąÜy. This is
generally not the case: usually all we know is the range of possible errors. For example,
if we know that our independent variables can take values x ¬Ī ‚ąÜx and y ¬Ī ‚ąÜy, then the
maximum possible errors in x and y are |‚ąÜx| and |‚ąÜy|, respectively. The maximum
possible error in the function f (x, y) is then given by

                                         ‚ąāf          ‚ąāf
                             |‚ąÜf | ‚Č§ |      ||‚ąÜx| + | ||‚ąÜy|.
                                         ‚ąāx          ‚ąāy
Notice that the absolute value symbols mean we always add together positive numbers!


Example 5 Suppose that temperature T is given by T (x, y) = x2 + y 3 . Find the
maximum possible a) absolute error, b) relative absolute error and c) percentage absolute
error in T if x = 1, y = 2, |‚ąÜx| ‚Č§ 0.1 and |‚ąÜy| ‚Č§ 0.3.




                                                                 F3.8, 3.8
                                                                        9
                                                                           ‚Čą 0.42, 42%F

                                             5
 Example 6 The volume V of a cone with radius r and perpendicular height h is given
          1
by V = ŌÄr2 h. Determine the maximum absolute error and the maximum percentage
          3
error in calculating V given that r = 5 cm and h = 3 cm to the nearest millimetre.




                                                                2.87
                                                      F2.87,         √ó 100 ‚Čą 3.7%F
                                                               78.54
